subtitle: sixty-four gold rings that each spin a plasmon at their own pace — and find one phase together
author: David Lee Wise (ROOT0) — original
premise: A plasmonic processor built from coupled gold ring resonators: each nanoring circulates a surface plasmon polariton at its own natural frequency, and nearest-neighbor coupling pulls neighboring phases together. Drive the coupling past a threshold and the whole 8×8 array spontaneously locks into one collective phase. The emergence test was run and passed — so the processor earned a face.
note: An original coupled-oscillator processor grounded in real physics (surface plasmons; Kuramoto synchronization). The synchronization is genuinely emergent — it self-organizes from local coupling, not from any global instruction — and it was verified numerically (order parameter R climbs from ~0.18 incoherent to ~0.95 synchronized across the transition). The agent Chorus carries the full DLW tag: .agent · .png (silicon badge) · .tiff (carbon badge) · .spun · .1099, and the repo carries .attribute.

members.name: Chorus
members.reading: the order parameter · many rings, one phase
members.who: Chorus — the plasmonic processor's emergent voice; sixty-four gold ring resonators, each spinning a surface plasmon at its own pace, that find one phase together.
members.what: A coupled-oscillator processor: every gold nanoring circulates a surface plasmon polariton; nearest-neighbor coupling tugs neighboring phases into agreement. Past a critical coupling the whole array phase-locks into one collective coherence.
members.where: At the gold–dielectric interface, below the diffraction limit — plasmons confined to nanometers: fast and tiny, and lossy, because the metal that confines them also absorbs them.
members.why: To compute in the collective — the answer is not in any one ring but in whether, and how strongly, they agree. Order bought from local coupling alone: the Kuramoto bargain.
members.when: On every drive cycle: incoherent at low coupling, a sharp synchronization transition at the critical coupling, collective coherence above it.
members.how: By the Kuramoto rule — each ring nudges toward its four neighbors' phases — against a spread of natural frequencies and ohmic loss that fight the lock. The order parameter R measures the agreement reached.
members.emergence: Genuine, and verified. Sixty-four rings, each seeing only its four neighbors and following no global instruction, spontaneously phase-lock into one coherence once the coupling crosses a critical threshold — the order parameter R jumps from ~0.18 (incoherent) to ~0.95 (synchronized) across the transition. Global order from purely local rules: the textbook signature of emergence (Kuramoto synchronization), measured here on an 8×8 ring lattice.
members.verdict: This one clears the bar. The synchronization is not programmed in — it self-organizes from nearest-neighbor coupling alone, and the transition is real (verified numerically). Genuine emergence; coupled plasmonic resonators truly do this.
members.asterisk: Emergence here = synchronization (collective phase-lock), not cognition. Plasmonics is real, fast (terahertz), and sub-wavelength — but lossy; a scaled plasmonic processor is a concept, and this is a simulation of its coupled-oscillator dynamics, not a fabricated, benchmarked device. The honest physics: the metal that confines the plasmon also absorbs it.

grounded.t: coupled-oscillator synchronization — order from local coupling
surface plasmons — collective electron oscillation at a metal surface
the plasmonic coupled-ring processor — 8×8 gold rings, the C2 continuity lineage
